Defense giving up big plays vs 12 play drives
stories.summary:
This topic drives me crazy. 
	 



	Heard it addressed on Locked On this morning.



	 



	”Well, you wanted a defense that gets after it, but with that comes giving up big plays.. McDermott’s defense didn’t do that.”  Summarizing a point made by Jerry O.  And he’s not wrong, but… 



	 



	Ok, cool… What his Defense did was allow teams to bleed clock, wear down our defense, convert third downs and then eventually score.   Thus, shortening games and putting more pressure on Josh Allen to score when he has the ball.  All the while operating in a run-heavy structure to protect the defense, that bleeds the clock, limits his pass attempts and makes him press to do something with it when he drops back.  
	 



	I understand the analytics of it.. and the fact is, drives that have an explosive play in them are far more likely to end in a score than those without them.   Noted. 
	 



	However, with the new kickoff rules and teams starting so much closer to midfield, I will take a defense that gives up 2-3 more big plays per game, as long as they are creating 6-8+ big plays themselves… turnovers, sacks, tfl’s etc. 



	 



	Case in point.  Denver playoff game.  Josh Allen spazzing before half.  Why did he do that?   It’s simple.. he saw an entire playoff half go by where he was almost perfect, but because of a James Cook fumble and the Defense not getting off the field - therefore limiting his drive chances - he was heading into halftime down.   So he pressed, and made it worse.  McDermott’s defensive philosophy affecting Josh Allen in a nutshell. 
	 



	The 3rd down stops and big plays for our Defense have to be there, but as long as they are.. everyone downvote me the first time I whine about a 65 yard explosive given up by our Defense in a Gamethread.  
	 



	Bend, don’t break only works when you’re actually bending and not breaking and not affecting your own QB while his counterpart elite QB’s don’t just break your defense when it matters.. they massacre it.

Jammerz One & Only Off-Season GMFAD
stories.summary:
Before I start just wanted to say there will be some important and tough decisions to be made. Mainly cap decisions. Big one is letting Alex Tuch go. It’s for the best. With our emerging young forwards it just doesn’t make any real sense to tie up around $10-$11 mill in one forward who is good for what 60-66 points? Even during the season if he didn’t get hot late he would’ve ended with 20 some goals and 50 some points. That much cap in a guy who will be 30 before the season starts and is already trending in the wrong direction isn’t what this franchise needs. This is literally a setup for another Jeff Skinner type contract debacle, to which we are still suffering with. 



Also Bowen Byram and his agent are at it again so yup he’s gone. In my perfect off-season I would love to re-sign Byram to a $9 mill contract but that’s not what he wants. He wants to be the de facto  #1D. He won’t get that here. He’s a must trade this offseason. Waiting and waiting is just going to be him waving goodbye without anything in return. 



Some. young players are ready to take the next step while some need a new type of guidance IMHO so some of the overpriced guys will be traded in my perfect offseason. 



The moves I’m making are mainly for both now and the future. My main philosophy is to be more defensive, get more and more and more physical and bring in a #1 centre. This will be a team made for the playoffs. Oh yes Helenius is well and ready to take over our #2 centre duties. 



My main goals for the offseason is get rid of the players that don’t want to be here, keep an eye out on our future, add high energy go go players, add a #1 centre, add leadership, add very physical players. 



Coaching & Management 



Fire Marty Wilford 



Need a full Lindy coaching staff. Someone who he’s trusted before. 



Replacement – James Patrick 



James comes into take over for Lindy after his 2 seasons but until then he will be the second in command. This guy is huge for young player development and he knows the ropes already. This is more of a 2 years down the road hiring so nobody else nabs him like the Isles did with McKee. 



Fire Seth Appert 



If Appert even remotely put together a good PP our team’s offense would be top 2-3 in the NHL. 



Replacement (one of) – Jessica Campbell or Vinny Prospal. 



Kraken did not utilize Jessica Campbell at all. Her specialty was the PP but the Kraken mainly just used her in a skating coach and skills development way. I think she’d be a great coach for our PP. 



I had Vinny Prospal as our next PP coach but he was sniped by the Blues. 



Fire Mark Jakubowski



Mark has been here since the Tim Murray days. He’s started here as AGM then to VP of Hockey Ops then back to AGM. Being part of that drought means he was part of some awful teams that weren’t ran all that well and high majority of the picks turned out to be nothing. 



Replacement – Shane Doan 



Let’s make this a family affair. Outside of the obvious he does bring a lot to the table. Is looking for a bigger role inside an organization and I think this would be the right fit for him. It’d be nice to see someone like Shane Doan join the organization in the front office. 



Re-Signings



Zach Benson 7 years @ $6.95 mill per



Notes



Exact same deal as Doaner. They’re worth pretty much the same. Obviously getting back a huge player for the long haul. 



Beck Malenstyn  5 years @ $3.3 mill per



Notes



This is a no brainer. He is our 4th line. This is the only UFA I’m re-signing. Yes that means bye to Tuch. Trust me you’ll thank me later. 



Trades



1st Trade 



To St. Louis Blues



Alex Tuch’s Rights



Josh Norris 



Bo Byram



Brodie Ziemer



20th Overall



To Buffalo Sabres



Robert Thomas 



Jordan Kyrou



Torey Krug



11th Overall



Notes 



We finally land our #1 centre. He adds a lot. Great on the PP. Great on the face-off dot. Most importantly the guy can play more than 40 games a season. Norris is just too injury prone to be an effective player for this team. He was also very underwhelming in the playoffs. 



We get a good offensive winger in Kyrou and we move up to the 11th overall pick. Kyrou can also play every forward position and is good defensively, is fast in transition and fits great with the current team. Kyrou can switch to centre if Helenius does indeed struggle as 2C (but I don’t see that happening.)



We also take on Krug’s last season as a cash (notice I said cash not cap) help for the Blues. He’s LTIR so we are just paying him. 



2nd Trade 



To Winnipeg Jets



Ukko-Pekka Luukkonen



Devon Levi



Jack Quinn



Peyton Krebs



Jordan Greenway 



2027 1st Round Pick



To Buffalo Sabres



Connor Hellebuyck



Notes 



Here we acquire one of the best goalies in the league. Jets have no future or present in goaltending if they trade Hellebuyck so in this deal they go with a UPL/Levi tandem. UPL being their short term answer and Levi being their long term answer. This is the right time to trade UPL. There’s a good chance his numbers stumble yet again after a solid yet short campaign. 



3rd Trade



To Edmonton Oilers



Alex Lyon 



To Buffalo Sabres



2026 2nd Round Draft Pick



Notes



We honestly have to get rid of this 3 goalie thing. Edmonton is desperate. Strike while the iron is hot with Lyon.  



4th Trade



To San Jose Sharks



Jason Zucker



Vsevolod Komarov



2026 2nd Round Draft Pick (Edmonton)



To Buffalo Sabres



Joshua Ravensbergen 



Notes



Hated doing this one as Jason is a great guy to have in the locker room but he just doesn’t fit anymore and I don’t want him playing what’s left of his career in the press box. So I thought of the best possible place for him that’s decently close to his home. San Jose add a vet that adds around 45+ points. 



In this trade we add our future number 1 goalie in Joshua Ravensbergen. This kid’s the real deal folks. San Jose has Askarov thus that makes Ravensbergen expendable. Once Hellebuyck’s contract is through he and Ellis should be ready to go and compete as a tandem. 



Draft



I’m only doing the first pick for us in this GMFAD. So without further ado the hometown Buffalo Sabres are proud to select from the United States Development Program LW/RW Wyatt Cullen, son of former NHLer Matt Cullen. 



Scouting Report 




Tremendous skater and has incredible speed



Very elusive player 



His vision is one of the tops in the class



High hockeyIQ



Great at both playmaking and scoring



He lives to drive offensive play



Great hands



Would be a great PP guy at the NHL level if developed the right way



That’s all about his offensive game, his defensive game well let’s just say it’s mainly a work in progress. 




Free Agency 



Sign Jacob Trouba to 4 years @ $6.5 mill per season. 



Notes



We replace Byram with Trouba. Here we add a very good two way defenseman. Great on the PK, great defensive game, great shot blocking abilities, can contribute offensively when needed and he is a beast that just loves to kick ass. We lose bit on the offensive side of things with Byram out and Trouba being his replacement but the gain in physicality, defensiveness and leadership makes it all worth it. 



Sign AJ Greer to 5 years @ $3.5 mill per season. 



Notes 



We add another unreal bottom 6 player who hits, has a high intensity forecheck, kills penalties, and a motor that just won’t stop. Think Malenstyn but for our LW. He can fill in on the 3rd line if an injury occurs.



Sign Jake Bean to 1 year @ $1.1 mill per season 



Notes



Just a depth signing. Mobile defenseman that can play the PP if injuries occur. 



Lineup



Forwards



Benson – Thomas – Thompson



Kyrou – Helenius – Doan



Kulich – McLeod – Ostlund



Greer – Carrick – Malenstyn 



Danforth/Kozak



Defense



Samuelsson – Dahlin



Power – Trouba



Metsa – Timmins



Johnson/Bean



Goalies



Hellebuyck 



Ellis



Note



I’m very sorry if this is somewhat messed up with spacing and word size and everything. I do not have a computer right now but hopefully you guys enjoyed the read. I know unrealistic but a man can wish.

stories.title: Road to the Draft, Rumors, Trades and Signings
stories.summary:
The Sabres Need to Jump on Things NOW



The trade of Michael Kesselring wasn’t unexpected but it was disappointing to move off this player so quickly. Injuries derailed his season and he never secured favorability with head coach Lindy Ruff. The Sabres essentially dumped his salary to move up 7 spots in the draft, probably for better trade capital. Hopefully GMJK makes a big splash on draft weekend because there is a real chance Buffalo will have no picks in the first three rounds if that top pick is on the move.



We all know the Alex Tuch situation. It appears highly probable he has played his final games as a Buffalo Sabre. If I’m Jarmo, a sign and trade attempt should be in the works. Don’t let him walk for nothing, make every attempt to collect some sort of asset for him. A second round pick, a bottom six defenseman, get something. If this reality isn’t enough of a downer, word on the street is that Bo Byram is not willing to extend with Buffalo. He has his agent in his ear and big money dollar signs swirling around his head. The Sabres can’t possibly take the Tuch approach again, HOPING he will come around this upcoming season. They must be proactive and use Byram as their top trade piece. 









At this point, I feel the Sabres should jump on the signings of their own players, and pronto. Don’t leave anything to chance. Leave Tuch and Byram in the rearview mirror at this point. (at least for now) Zach Benson is the new priority and Beck Malenstyn is essential as well. I would love to get Peyton Krebs signed too for a reasonable cost but he could be a trade asset now. I hope not. Take care of in-house business in the next week while still on the phone looking for deals as well. Get the rest of your own players secured then go from there. They can circle back to Tuch and Byram.



Players of interest for me include big winger Matthew Knies and his cost controlled contract. He is in year two of a six year contract for $7.75 million per season. He could easily slide into the top six, if not the top line. Defender Zach Whitecloud could play in the 4, 5 or 6 spot with Byram gone. He has loads of experiencee and is signed for two more years at a very reasonable $2.75 million per year. Robert Thomas remains the big elusive fish and the cost to acquire him would be the most expensive. That is, if the Blues are still listening.

stories.title: Sabres trade Kesselring to Sharks to move up seven spots in 2026 Draft
stories.summary:
The Buffalo Sabres have set the ball rolling on what could be a momentous summer for the franchise by trading away defenseman Michael Kesselring and their 27th-overall pick in the upcoming NHL Draft to San Jose Sharks, and getting back the 20th-overall pick. 



The Sharks had obtained that pick from the Edmonton Oilers for defenseman Jake Walman in March 2025. 



Kesselring only featured in 34 games with the Sabres with injuries limiting his playing time and utility for Buffalo. He was arguably the centerpiece of the trade that brought him and Josh Doan to Western New York from the Utah Mammoth, but was overshadowed in impact by the son of star defenseman forward Shane Doan. He had two assists and averaged 13:24 of ice time.



The defenseman was at the end of his two-year extension that he had signed with Utah and was set to be an RFA this summer. 



The NHL Draft will be held at KeyBank Center on Friday, June 26 and Saturday, June 27, and the Sabres have four picks: Round 1 (20th overall), Round 4, Round 5 & Round 6.

stories.title: State of the Sabres, Checklisting Areas of Concern
stories.summary:
Playoffs Over, Draft Fast Approaching, Jarmo Will Be Busy and Tested



Today we examine seven points of interest for Sabres fans as business will ramp up for the team and the NHL over the next several weeks. Buffalo has areas of concern they must handle correctly. Opinions in this article are my own, it would be a boring world if we all thought as one.



1. Alex Tuch



Mr. Buffalo Sabre himself is the big topic of the offseason for Buffalo. At this point, it feels like he will most assuredly test the free agent market. Some still feel like a last minute deal could be hammered out with the Sabres. No matter what happens, color me disappointed that one of the biggest faces of the franchise is going this route. I know it is his right and by NHL standards he is looking to maximize his earnings and it’s his career path and all that jazz. I’m just not seeing any compromise from Tuch’s camp. Pressing for more money when there is life-changing millions on the table already just never sits well with me. The Sabres presented a fair offer I feel, and they are surely willing to inch it up a little bit more to keep him. Tuch and his upstart teammates are in a good spot with Buffalo now, ending the curse and putting the kibosh on the dreadful playoff drought at long last. The future looks bright and Alex could be a well paid part of it, but his exit could really set the team back. There should be some concessions on both sides to get a deal done. For an average Joe struggling in today’s world, with skyrocketing expenses, it’s difficult to sympathize with millionaire negotiations. Sorry, that’s just me and my perspective. By all accounts, Buffalo is prepared to double Tuch’s salary at 9 to 9.5 million and offer him 7 years of term at least. That doesn’t seem good enough to Alex and his agent right now. If his family/living status is a part of this decision, well, there is not much Buffalo can do besides overpay wildly to keep him. That might not even do it. Growing up in nearby Syracuse and being a fan of the team as a child does not seem like a factor any more. The money and the term is paramount, along with his family. (with his west-coast wife being a possible influence)



Please don’t get me wrong, I love having Alex Tuch on the team and I hope they can sign him. It’s the staggering amount of money involved to retain our player and his apparent stubberness to not bend just a little that rubs me the wrong way. It’s not just Tuch for the record, he is just the current big name player in this position. Buffalo is a blue-collar town that just emerged from 14 years of misery with a division championship. You would think Mr. Sabre himself would want to remain a part of the resurgence and figure this out for the good of both sides.



General manager Jarmo Kekäläinen has his work cut out for him. How the Tuch situation plays out will directly effect Buffalo’s other moves. That will lay the groundwork for the Sabres’ off season. With Jeff Skinner’s biggest buyout hiccup coming in 2026-27, managing next year while fitting Tuch in is Jarmo’s biggest hurtle. If he can work it, Tuch’s new contract won’t be as much of a hindrance after that. Especially with the NHL’s salary cap going up more and more. Next season is the biggest and toughest obstacle for the new Buffalo GM.



In today’s NHL climate, teams must generally overpay for free agents, including their own. Not many players do the hometown discount anymore, even in ideal conditions. If the Sabres retain Tuch’s services, the likelyhood of him earning that money probably tops off at 5 more years. That is something Buffalo will have to deal with down the road. For now, what gives you the best chance to continue to win? Alex Tuch, most assuredly. But paying and keeping one of your own top players means enduring the loss of others teammates to make it happen. The BIG MONEY/sometimes overpaid stars of the league don’t seem to realize this or seem to care. Your team will take a hit to the talent on the roster in order to pay you mega-bucks to make sure you are happy, so I hope you are happy. Or is letting Tuch go at this point and exploring other options through trade or in-house promotions the move to make? That money saved can be used elsewhere if Jarmo can swing a deal. Buffalo had an eye out for a number one center upgrade already, adding a suitable replacement for number 89 as well will be a monumental task.









2. Benny, Beck, Byram and Krebs



These four players all require attention too, with Benson, Malenstyn and Krebs needing new contracts and Bowen Byram needing a contract extention. Any deal with Bo will thankfully not impact this year’s salary cap. Hopefully Double B liked the progression of the team and his role among the other players and is willing to extend. Otherwise, he is likely a main trade piece to aquire other top talent.



Meanwhile, Zach Benson is a must to sign. Buffalo would be wise to lock him up in a long term deal of at least six years. I originally had Taz signing a deal a little north of Josh Doan’s recent new contract. I think now it will be even more north than that after his playoffs. Even with the added expense, Benny is worth it. I like to imagine the second half of his deal being a bargain for the results he will put forth in four to six years.



Beck Malenstyn is a must signing to me as well. Something in the neighborhood of 3 years and 3.25 million per for Buffalo’s stud 4th liner. The guy is a workhorse and a great shot blocker. He is also physical and lays the body and it’s his efforts that make the 4th line go, even with interchangeable parts. I very much hope to retain Beck and his services. Buffalo has some solid bottom line pieces as cheap labor to offset his contract in Tyson Kozak, Justin Danforth and Sam Carrick.



Peyton Krebs is always a polarizing player around here but I love the guy. He enjoys being a Buffalo Sabre, he has a great work ethic and is an underrated leader for this team. I would like to see him in a third line role, as we saw some solid offensive production this past year. He has a strong two way game and is a key piece of the puzzle to me. Where his contract negotiations go, I don’t really know. He has been a solid, inexpensive worker-bee these past four years and deserves a raise. Hopefully that bump up in pay is still affordable for the team.



Mar 22, 2026; Anaheim, California, USA; Buffalo Sabres left wing Beck Malenstyn (29) controls the puck against the Ahaheim Ducks during the third period at Honda Center. Mandatory Credit: Gary A. Vasquez-Imagn Images



3. Sacrifices



If Tuch and company are to be signed, some of the higher priced, expendable players will need to be moved. Jason Zucker and Jordan Greenway are two top candidates, unfortunately. Between them, that is almost 9 million in cap space that could be cleared out for Tuch in particular. Both would be a jolting loss for the team, though not irreplaceable. Zucker may have been one of former GM Kevyn Adams’ best ever signing. The veteran winger scored 45 goals and nearly 100 points in two seasons with the Blue and Gold. He is also a power play specialist on a team with a much maligned man advantage. Zucker would surely be missed but Buffalo has young replacement players in house to fill his skates.



Jordan Greenway could also be on his way out if the Sabres can find somebody to manage his contract. The big man is a strong penalty killer, sound defensively and is Buffalo’s only real legitimate fighter. I’m sure the Sabres would love to keep him, at a more reasonable 2.5 million a year and not the hefty 4 million he currently makes. Alex Tuch’s stance and the RFAs will make that near impossible. 



Another potential casualty could be center Ryan McLeod and his 5 million per season deal. I don’t even want to discuss this option because McLeod to me is a vital part of the team. Elite quickness in his skating, solid two-way play and over 100 points for Buffalo in Ryan’s first two seasons. The Sabres paid up for him too with a strong prospect in Matthew Savoie sent back to Edmonton in return. I don’t want to see McLeod dispatched so quickly. I believe he still has a part to play in the Sabres’ resurgence.



Nov 5, 2024; Buffalo, New York, USA;  Ottawa Senators left wing Brady Tkachuk (7) and Buffalo Sabres left wing Jordan Greenway (12) fight during the first period at KeyBank Center. Mandatory Credit: Timothy T. Ludwig-Imagn Images



4. The Draft (First round, Friday, June 26th)



The live event is to be held in Buffalo again (first time since 2016) but the hometown team might not even have a selection in round one. Or round two either. (already traded to Ottawa) It all depends on Jarmo Kekäläinen’s wheelings and dealings. If the Sabres press hard for a number one center which has been speculated, Buffalo’s first overall selection in the draft will surely be in play. It is safe to say the team is in WIN NOW mode so Jarmo’s motives will no doubt reflect that. The GM nearly dealt 2025 first rounder Radim Mrtka already and could use him as trade bait again.



Buffalo’s big splash on draft day may well be a big trade for an experienced here and now player. I would estimate there is better than a 50-50 chance of that happening. But the deal might not be there, or the cost too great. Perhaps a move is made before or after the draft, anything is possible. While Buffalo’s 1st rounder may be up for grabs, there is an interesting variety of players that could be available if they keep the pick.



Brooks Rogowski, C, Oshawa (OHL) The hulking forward is Tage Thompson sized at 6’7″ but uses his big frame more to his advantage than TNT does. He is tough to defend, especially around the net and rarely loses puck battles. Rogowski had 42 points (15 goals, 27 assists) in 46 games this season and is committed to play at Michigan State this Fall.



Jack Hextall, C, Youngstown (USHL) The right-handed shot, high-end forward had 58 points (20 goals, 38 assists) in 59 games this season. Hextall is more of a playmaker, but he did find the net more often as the season went on. He has a strong two-way game already which should serve him well in the pros. The 6 foot, 188 pounder is also committed to Michigan State next season.



Maddox Dagenais, C, Quebec (QMJHL) If this talented and sometimes physical player is still avalable, Buffalo should highly consider him. He will almost assuredly be snatched up by Montreal with the next selection if the Sabres should pass. In 62 games, the 18 year old had 62 points (30 goals, 32 assists) with an impressive 25 power play points. Dagenais projects to be a solid number 2 center in the NHL.



Gleb Pugachyov, RW, Russia (KHL) This 6’3″ 200 pound Russian winger strikes fear into his opponents, literally. They are highly aware when he is on the ice and must keep their heads on a swivel. This power forward has a physical dominance not often seen these days as heavy contact is a big part of his game. He is not without skill, but he is more of a crash the net type of player. (never a bad thing) He is projected as a late first, early second round selection that Buffalo should have on their radar. Pugachyov is one of those players every other team craves and I really feel like the Sabres should take a closer look here.









5. Trading Places



A number one center and/or a possible Tuch replacement are the biggest upgrades/needs for the Sabres that will require dealing outside of the organization. Robert Thomas of the Blues seems to still be a favorite potential acquisition for Buffalo fans. St. Louis seemingly put a stop to the Thomas rumor mill, stating he would still be on the Blues’ roster in the Fall. They are NOT actively shopping him. Frankly, we have heard things like that before from teams with available players. Doing that relaxes one of your own and drives up the potential price tag, in theory. I feel St. Louis is still listening and the right offer could land the first round pick from 2017. Thomas turns 27 next month and has loads of experience and success. Through 530 NHL games, the Blues center has scored 460 points. His career high for goals is only 26 but he has notched 60 assists in a season twice.



Also in St. Louis, right winger Jordan Kyrou remains a possibility as a Tuch replacement, should he depart. Kyrou has scored 70 plus points 3 of the past 5 years and is a pretty consistent offensive threat. He doesn’t have Tuch’s defense and penalty killing ability though, so his overall game in not on the same level. Kyrou is a year older than Thomas and more likely to be moved.



Elsewhere, center Mason McTavish of the Ducks could be an option. McTavish had a falling off this season in points and with the team in general. He is a talented 23 year old, with 181 points through 304 NHL games. He had a career high 52 points the previous season (22g, 30a) but dropped off to 42 this past year. He still has room to grow and might benefit from a new enviroment. His defensive game also needs some improvement.



Another recent idea involves New Jersey stud Nico Hischier. The 27 year old center has been a leader for the Devils for years now. He has good history with Lindy Ruff, who named him team captain. Hischier has nearly 500 points through just over 600 NHL games. He is signed for one more year with a cap hit of $7,250,000. That could work for next season, with some salary going back New Jersey’s way in any transaction. If the Sabres were to acquire Hischier though, it would need to be a sign and trade acquisition, with him agreeing to a new contract with Buffalo. A substantial one. Any new Nico Hischier deal will be north of 11 million per season. Maybe even 12 or more. The Sabres would have a full year to make it work as that contract won’t kick in until 2027-28. They would avoid Jeff Skinnner’s biggest year of payout too. Hischier could also be a good mentor/influence on our young up and coming European players as well.



There is also a chance Jarmo goes off the board and is after a player nobody has in mind. That might actually be wise, as he would have little to no competition in bargaining. If he shocks us like that, we all hope it is a good shocking.



Mar 21, 2026; Vancouver, British Columbia, CAN;  St. Louis Blues center Robert Thomas (18) skates with the puck against Vancouver Canucks left wing Liam Ohgren (92) during the third period at Rogers Arena. Mandatory Credit: Simon Fearn-Imagn Images



6. The Young Core



The trio of Jiri Kulich, Noah Ostlund and Konsta Helenius all have a good chance of being in Buffalo’s top nine in the Fall of 2026. As far as I’m concernned, Ostlund has already graduated to the big club. In 60 games last season, he registered 27 points and was a +11. Noah looked the part with elite playmaking abilities and shifty stickhandling. I can see him scoring 50 points this year. The Sabres really missed him in the playoffs after he fell with injury just three games in against Boston. Ostlund should be right back on the team this season from my perspective. Kulich graduated too in my eyes, playing 62 games in 2024-25 (with 24 points) and starting last season in Buffalo as well before being pulled out of the lineup with his blood clot issues. By all accounts, he underwent a successful procedure at the Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota and is doing well now. He anticipates making a full return to the lineup for the upcoming season. As some have suggested, Jiri might spend some time with the Amerks to really get back in professional shape again. That is possible. But he has summer training and Sabres’ training camp to show he is ready to go. I think he will be.



Now, onto Konsta Helenius. While Kulich and Ostlund seem to have spots waiting for them, Konsta may have forced his way in with his strong post season cameo. (and his play in Rochester) That would make three talented wiz kids on the roster. By appearing in the playoffs for Buffalo, (and providing a needed spark) his entry-level contract (ELC) year was officially burned. As a result, the clock on his contract advanced, meaning he will reach restricted free agency one year earlier than if he had stayed in the minors. (or just didn’t play) So as far as I’m concerned, that means his time is now. Ostlund and Kulich have the better NHL experience but Helenius has the highest ceiling in my opinion. As we saw against Montreal in the post season, he looks NHL ready. Having all three in the lineup not only injects youthful talent, it also helps Buffalo save on cap space. That will be huge, depending on what happens with Tuch and the RFA’s. Plus any big trade that might go down, bringing in a high salary player. Zucker, Greenway, maybe even Jack Quinn might be involved in Jarmo’s dealings. The wiz kids provide adequate replacement level talent on the cheap, for this season anyway.



Of course, GMJK could also involve one of the youngins’ I have just discussed here in a trade too. I sure hope not, I have high hopes for all three. Helenius should be off the table for sure, Jarmo!



Jan 27, 2026; Toronto, Ontario, CAN; Buffalo Sabres forward Noah Ostlund (86) controls the puck against Toronto Maple Leafs defenseman Troy Stetcher (28) during the second period at Scotiabank Arena. Mandatory Credit: John E. Sokolowski-Imagn Images



7. Free Agency



Lastly, we come to free agency. One of the best players available is one of Buffalo’s own. And by recent reports, the Sabres and Tuch’s camp are still far apart. Unless there is a last minute miracle or Buffalo is able to pull off a sign and trade deal for some value back, Alex could depart with the Sabres having nothing to show for it. The 2026 free agency class is widely considered to be one of the weakest and shallowest in recent memory. I don’t see many good options for the Blue and Gold. You can expect many GMs to hand out major contracts to older players who arguably do not deserve lengthy, high-money deals, leading some teams to pursue blockbuster trades rather than overpaying free agents. That is where the Sabres probably fall.



If Buffalo does trade out a valuable but highly paid player like Greenway, the Sabres could use free agency to pick up a gritty, hard-hitting and willing to drop the gloves player for a savings. They especially might do this if the team passes on the physical Josh Dunne too, who is a UFA. If these two are gone next season, the Sabres would be very low on players who can handle themself when sh*t goes down. It doesn’t matter if you don’t like fighting in the game or not, every team needs at least one guy for the occasion.



Ross Johnston fits the bill for Buffalo. He is a 6’5″ 230 pound winger who played for the Ducks the last two seasons after many years with the Islanders. Johnston is a tough hombre’ who had four official fights last season, with many other skirmishes. The veteran is 32 years old. Another option is the monster that is Curtis Douglas. The 26 year old is 6’9″ and 242 pounds. He made his NHL debut last season, spliting time with the Canucks and goon-squad Tampa Bay Lightning. Douglas had a whopping 10 fights last season in only 43 games and would certainly be an intimidation factor. Neither of these gritty, tough guys would be regular players in the lineup, I imagine. But they would make solid, plug and play players used to face the right team though. Either bruiser could be had for the low-low price of $1.5 to $1.75 million per season.



Beyond some fill-in grit, I don’t see the Sabres making a big splash in free agency. In fact, I mostly hope they avoid it. The right big deal via a trade or two is the way to go, if Jarmo can swing it. And with that last thought, this b*tch of an article is now over. Those are your biggest areas of concern for Buffalo, a full plate for the team to handle this summer if they want a return visit to the playoffs. Commence the discussion.

stories.title: 2025-26 Stanley Cup Final Open Thread
stories.summary:
The Buffalo Sabres return to the postseason didn’t go all the way to the Stanley Cup Finals like so many of us had hoped it would. 



Instead we have an expected and heavily favored Eastern Conference champions Carolina Hurricanes who have breezed through with a 12-1 record taking on a surprise quantity Vegas Golden Knights who got hot at the right time and then knocked out the overwhelming favorite and Presidents Trophy winners Colorado Avalanche in a sweep. 



Sabres fans have reason to dislike both teams and I won’t judge you for assuming hockey season is over. 



2026 Stanley Cup Final schedule



Game 1: Vegas 5, Carolina 4



Game 2: Carolina 4, Vegas, 3 (OT)



Game 3: Vegas 5, Carolina 4 (2OT)



Game 4: Carolina 5, Vegas, 3



Game 5: Carolina 4, Vegas, 2



Game 6: Carolina at Vegas, 8 p.m. ET, Sunday, June 14 (ABC, SN, CBC, TVAS)



*Game 7: Vegas at Carolina, 8 p.m. ET, Wednesday, June 17 (ABC, SN, CBC, TVAS)​



* – If necessary

stories.title: Letter Grades for the Players, 2025-26 Season – Part 2 of 2
stories.summary:
After Great Team Success, How Do the Sabres Break Down Individually?



Today we take a look at the individual performances of each player and assign a letter grade accordingly. Each Sabre graded must meet the 20 games played with Buffalo minimum. So no Konsta Helenius or Sam Carrick and several others. These grades are for the regular season only, not playoffs. We will split the eligible players into two randomly listed sections. This is part 2 of 2.




Josh Doan (82-25-27-52 -4) Grade A




Of the many pleasant surprises this season, Josh Doan’s performance stands out. He went from the other player acquired for Peterka to a vital cog in the Sabres’ offensive machine. He set career highs in games played, goals, assists, and points. His relentless forechecking and continuous motor made him a favorite in the locker room and throughout the fanbase.




Alex Tuch (79-33-33-66 +24) Grade B+




The pending UFA had another quality season for Buffalo. Tuch was effective in all situations and notched his third 30+ goal season in his last four. His 66 points were second among Sabre forwards and is rightfully due for a significant raise. If this was his last season in Buffalo, he was a big reason this team ended the draught though his consistent high-end production.




Mattias Samuelsson (78-13-28-41 +41) Grade A+




Talk about a complete 180. Last season, many (including myself) were calling for Samuelsson’s contract to be bought out. His response was to set career highs in just about every statistical category and become one of the best defensive defensemen in the league. He’s shown he belongs on the top pair with Dahlin and should be a fixture there for years to come.




Peyton Krebs (82-12-27-39 +13) Grade B




Krebs has turned into a quality utility man for Buffalo. He played primarily on either the 1st or 4th lines and found success in both roles. In particular, his ability to play well enough with Thompson/Tuch on the first line allowed Buffalo to maintain balanced lines despite injuries to some of their better forwards. Long term, Krebs probably fits best as a bottom 6 forward who can move up in a pinch.  




Josh Norris (44-13-21-34 +11) Grade C+




Another injury plagued season limited Norris’ impact. At the top of his game, he provides high end speed and skill. However, his lack of availability is a real concern. He has the talent to be a game-changer on a nightly basis, but this season left fans underwhelmed. Hopefully, he can stay healthy long enough to make his mark in the league.




Noah Ostlund (60-11-16-27 +11) Grade A




Ostlund earned a full-time role in Buffalo this season and is clearly one of Lindy Ruff’s favorite players. He elevates his linemates and possesses elite playmaking abilities. Beyond his on-ice impact, his unceremonious demotion to Rochester in December seemed to be the straw that broke the camel’s back for Kevyn Adams’ tenure as GM which completely changed the season’s trajectory. Ostlund has staked his claim as a key young player on the roster with tremendous potential for growth.




Conor Timmins (39-0-8-8 -8) Grade C




Timmins’ season had a good start before his season was derailed by a broken leg in December. He was able to return a few weeks before the playoffs and continued to contribute, particularly on the penalty kill. He’s effective as a bottom pair defenseman but understandably lost some of his effectiveness post-injury. All-in-all, he met expectations considering his significant injury.




Tage Thompson (81-40-41-81 -6) Grade A




Thompson had another great season. He led the Sabres in points for the 4th time in 5 years and notched his 3rd 40 goal season in his last 4. He was an offensive force on a nightly basis and seemed to benefit from his move back to center. He had two separate 6 game goal streaks. He’s been the Sabres’ top forward for years and this season was no exception.




Tyson Kozak (46-2-4-6 -1) Grade C-




After splitting last season between Rochester and Buffalo, Kozak stayed on the NHL roster for the entire season. Injuries hampered what could have been a season he claimed the 4th line center role for himself. Instead, Sam Carrick was acquired at the trade deadline to upgrade the position. This leaves Kozak on the outside looking in without injuries to lineup regulars. When he plays, he shows tremendous speed and is defensively reliable. Unless something changes, his future in Buffalo looks to be a rotational depth forward.




Josh Dunne (34-1-3-4 -7) Grade C




When Josh Dunne was signed, most thought he would primarily play in Rochester. Due to injuries, he remained in Buffalo nearly the whole season. He played relatively well on the 4th line but his limitations became more pronounced against faster opponents. He struggled against teams like Montreal and Tampa Bay when the game speed cranked up a notch. He’s a good player to have as injury insurance but lacks effectiveness against top competition.




Ukko-Pekka Luukkonen (35 GP 22-9-3, 2.52 GAA .910 SV%, 1 SO) Grade B




UPL had a bounce back season and seemed to benefit from the season-long goaltending rotation. Although he played the least number of games for Buffalo since the 2022-2023 season, he posted a career best GAA and tied his career best save percentage. Consistency has gotten better but continues to be his Achillies heel. He provides the best upside for the current Sabre goalies but the valleys in his game can be tough to watch.

stories.title: 2026 NHL Entry Draft – T McGee’s Seven Round Mock
stories.summary:
We’re under a month away from the 2026 NHL Entry Draft, and it’s time to take a peek at some of the players that could fall to the Buffalo Sabres.Here’s a bumper seven-rounder from T McGee for your weekend reading.  



DBTB – 



A Seven Round Mock Draft?  In this economy?  Actually, I can’t stand that line, so let’s just say it was originally a Five Rounder but with inflation, it’s now Seven.  Not that anyone noticed.  No, all people want to talk about is the Sabres breaking the playoff drought…the Sabres winning a playoff series…almost winning a second one?  Losing in OT in Game 7?  That sounds about right.



Doesn’t anyone care about the children? 



Seriously though, I appreciate that maybe, maybe, the days of worrying for weeks and months about who the Sabres will draft is past.  As fans, we have bigger fish to fry.  And honestly, I loved every second of the playoff run.  But I, even in the thick of the Montreal series, still followed this Draft class, looking for diamonds in the rough.  Although I’ll be the first to admit, I’m not used to the Sabres drafting in the 20s…so this will take some getting used to!  Nevertheless, I hope some of you will find this snapshot of who might be around there and what the Sabres might do when their name is called to be useful.  



Interestingly, with Jarmo Kekkalainen in the GM’s seat, we don’t really know how he will approach the Draft.  Sure, we have several years from his tenure in Columbus, but most of his scouting staff is different in BFLO.  Jerry Forton is still at Harborcenter, masterminding the prospect analysis.  And the game is different even from just a few years ago.  We won’t know what Jarmo might do until they announce that the Sabres are on the clock.  What we do know is that he isn’t afraid of making an unorthodox move, or draft choice.  He’s got a bit of a history of doing just that.  It doesn’t go away just because a new team signs your paycheck.    



The one thing I think BFLO needs to look at is a high-skill, high-offensive output kind of player.  We know the pipeline is heavy with defensive D-Men (Strbak, Geary, Kleber, Mrtka, Novikov, and Bedkowski) and goalies (Ratzlaff, Levi, Prokhorov, Meloche, and Leenders).  But up front, most of their high-skill guys have graduated (Benson, Ostlund, Kulich) or are in the process of doing so (Helenius, Wahlberg).  Yeah, they have Brodie Ziemer coming but otherwise, right now, they’ve got some late-round surprises and some scrappy candidates who if they’re lucky will contend for a spot in the bottom six.  They need a new guy(s) who can play that high-octane game and who have the upside to score 20 goals (or more!) for them.  I’d also look for some defensively advanced, 2-way forwards that could slot in to the bottom 6, as many successful teams who’ve got their Top 6 locked in are looking for those 3rd and 4th liners as raw, young talent on cheap ELC contracts to relieve the Cap pressure (think Florida with guys like Samoskevich and Vilmanis, and going back away, Lundell).  The Sabres current prospect pool is heavily weighted toward Defense and Goal, so I would not prioritize those positions in this year’s Draft.  They need to reload on creative offensive forwards.        



Speaking of their young prospects, changes are afoot.  We’ve already seen Gavin McCarthy and Max Strbak, two promising young D-Men, end their collegiate careers and sign with Rochester.  There could be more coming this Summer…the aforementioned Brodie Ziemer missed the Frozen Four and had his coach receive a pink slip.  Jake Richard, Stivan Sardaryan, and Luke Osburn could also join the Amerks.  And Helenius, Komarov, Levi, Ratzlaff, and Wahlberg need all the help they can get!   



Meanwhile, Prokhor Poltapov, over in the KHL, has 6P in 10 playoff games for CKSA Moskva, a pretty impressive performance to finish 3rd on the team.  Fast-skating D-Man Noah Laberge has 13P in 12 playoff games for Newfoundland as he continues his breakout season.  Melvin Novotny just keeps piling up numbers for Muskegon in his first season in North America with 14P in 15 playoff games.  He’s facing off in the Clark Cup Final against Sioux City, the team of fellow Sabre draft pick Ashton Schultz.  Unfortunately, his season ended with 6P in 6 games before an injury derailed the rest of his playoff journey.  Meanwhile in the WHL, Radim Mrtka managed 3P in 5 games before being eliminated, then jumped right into the line-up in Rochester where he helped the Amerks take things to 5 games before they, too, were eliminated.  



In net big Sammy Meloche took the Huskies of Rouyn-Noranda to the QMJHL finals, finishing with a 2.52 GAA and .905 SV%…and in the OHL, Ryerson Leenders dazzled, doing all he could to prevent Jay McKee’s Brantford team getting upset, with a 2.41 GAA and .906 SV%.  But it was Russian tendy Yevgeni Prokhorov who impressed the most, continuing his outstanding season with a ridiculous 1.92 GAA and a .938 SV% in the MHL playoffs.  Last but not least, forgotten Swedish forward Joel Ratkovic has 14P in 13 games for his Karlskrona club in Swedish Allsvenskan/Ettan.  



As far as the Draft itself, it will take place in BFLO this year at the end of June!  So that’s pretty cool.  The Combine is due to begin next week (also in BFLO), so be on the lookout for that.  



Gavin McKenna has, in many ways, retaken his top spot on the Big Board.  His second half was sensational, with 33P in 18 games, he looked more confident and more involved off the puck.  That doesn’t mean Ivar Stenberg is a consolation prize.  He simply plays a different, more complete game than McKenna.  It would not shock me in the least if he went first off the Board to the Leafs.  They’ve got plenty of offense.  Adding a responsible forward with some elite skill wouldn’t be a bad choice.  Defensemen rule the Top Ten, but I think as high as #8 things start to get interesting where team needs start to take precedence over who is ranked higher.  Players like Viggo Bjorck, the Ruck Twins (Liam and Marcus), and dynamic center Wyatt Cullen have made first-round cases, while some players are sliding a bit this late in the year.  Guys like Mathis Preston, Ryan Roobroeck, Ty Lawrence and Luke Schairer have slid in my estimation…some out of the first round entirely, and perhaps even further.  



Just a reminder: the Sabres only have a scant 4 selections in this year’s Draft.  They haven’t been in that neighborhood since Kevyn Adams’ first year as GM, when he drafted Jack Quinn and JJ Peterka with his top 2 picks.  BFLO moved their 2nd in the Cozens – Norris trade, their 3rd and 6th in the Sam Carrick deal, and a 4th in the Stanley/Schenn trade.  They also used their 7th in the Tanner Pearson deal, I believe.  Anyways, I suppose if they move Tuch, Krebs or Byram they could recoup some or all of those picks, but as of right now, let’s mostly just stay with what they’ve got.  Yes, I cheated a bit…in the interest of getting another pick, I had the Sabres make a deal with Calgary to trade back.  For the sake of argument let’s say there was a player on the Board that the Flames really wanted, and BFLO needed more picks.  Voila!  



TRADE:  BUF #27 -> CAL #34, #55  



Dec 8, 2025; Calgary, Alberta, CAN; Calgary Flames and Buffalo Sabres players get into a scrum during the third period at Scotiabank Saddledome. Mandatory Credit: Sergei Belski-Imagn Images



Besides getting the Memorial Cup underway (Chicoutimi, Kitchener, host city Kelowna, and Everett battling it out!) and the USHL engaged in the Clark Cup Final, most league playoffs are wrapped up.  Now it’s on to the Combine and then teams will be putting together their final Big Boards leading up to the big day at the end of June.  You’ll be seeing one more, final Mock Draft and a final Big Board from me before Day One arrives.  



PS:  Players marked with ** = an over-ager, that is, a player who was not drafted in their first year of eligibility.  They can be one, two or three years over-age, but (unless the CBA has changed) once you get to four years past your original draft year, you become a UDFA.

7#12:  SEATTLE (NEW JERSEY):  Nick Koering, 6’5 LHD, USHL



7#13:  NY ISLANDERS: Cohen Bidgood, 6’0 W, OHL



7#14:  COLUMBUS: Vlad Poskurin, 6’1 G, RUS



7#15:  DETROIT:  Yan Melnikov, 5’10 W, RUS



7#16:  WASHINGTON: Thomas Rousseau, 5’11 LW, QMJHL



7#17:  LA KINGS: Boris Borin, 5’11 C, BCHL



7#18:  ANAHEIM:  Brian McFadden, 6’5 RHD, US HS 



7#19:  NASHVILLE (UTAH): Philip Larsson, 6’1 W, SWE



7#20:  EDMONTON:  Cruz Pavao, 6’0 W, WHL



7#21:  PHILADELPHIA: Pavel Martinu, 6’5 C, USHL 



7#22:  COLORADO (PITTSBURGH):  Darian Rolsing, 6’6 RHD, WHL



7#23:  COLORADO (OTTAWA):  Shawn Carrier, 5’10 W, QMJHL **



7#24:  BOSTON: Fabrice Bouvard, 6’1 LW, SWISS



7#25:  FLORIDA (MINNESOTA): Flo Houle, 6’1 RW, QMJHL**



7#26:  TAMPA BAY:  Logan Hawery, 5’10 C/W, OHL 



7#27:  WINNIPEG (BUFFALO): Rasmus Rinne, 5’11 W, FIN



7#28:  DALLAS: Joe Salandra, 5’10 W, OHL



7#29:  VEGAS:  Andrei Molgachyov, 5’9 W, WHL



7#30:  MONTREAL: Nick Bogas, 6’0 LHD, USHL



7#31:  MONTREAL (CAROLINA): Arseni Illyin, 6’2 W, RUS



7 #32:  LA KINGS (COLORADO):  Dylan Rozzi, 5’11 LW, QMJHL







SABRES’ HAUL: 



2#4: Jack Hextall, 6’0 C, USHL: 



The Sabres love high-motor, two-way players who can contribute on both ends (Benson, Helenius, Ostlund, Kulich, Ziemer).  They also love guys who can play down the middle.  Here they get both.  Hextall is an intense competitor with a nice combination of physicality, high-end skill, and a desire to win that should fit in well with the young players in the Sabres’ organization.  The main attribute that makes Hextall so effective is his head.  He has a ton of hockey smarts, which helps him recognize spacing, passing and shooting lanes, and anticipating his opponents’ decisions.  Sees things others don’t.  He plays a bit faster than he actually is, owing to his ability to read where the puck is going and his full commitment to go all out to get there ahead of anyone else.  



Those same smarts make him more attentive to the finer details of the game, the little things where he excels.  Stick lifts when fighting for puck, taking the right lanes to pressure puck carriers into turnovers, winning face-offs and finding a sliver of open space to get off a shot or a pass against defensive structure – he does it all.  Has a variety of skills that he can use from one possession to the next.  A crafty playmaker who varies his speed to keep defenders off-balance and maximize his options, is agile enough to beat his check to the net front, and once he gets there, he’s got the high-end hands to find and finish, tip or deflect pucks on their way to the goalie.  Pounces on mistakes without hesitation.  No qualms about driving to the net and getting into the scoring areas even while taking a beating in the process.  Versatile.  Can play a high-flying skill game or a grinding, methodical puck possession game, at either wing or center.  



Skating is very good, although he’s not great.  Can get caught slowing down his pace when things get chaotic, but his pace of play has improved by leaps and bounds as the season went on and is rarely a drawback for Hextall.  A pass-first player, has great vision and feel for the O-zone when the puck is on his stick, and when it isn’t, he goes right to the scoring areas to create trouble for the defense.  In his own end, he works hard and isn’t afraid to lay the body on his check or pin-and-hold the puck carrier until his teammates recover the puck.  Can get a little distracted at times in the D-zone, leaving the middle of the ice to pursue pucks or try to jump passing lanes, but that tendency started to decline as the year went on.  



Hexy put up an impressive 58P in 59 games for Youngstown in the USHL, finishing second among draft-eligibles, and comfortably between Sabre prospects Ryan Rucinski and Mel Novotny, so we know BFLO has seen plenty of him.  He also added 7P in 5 games for gold-medal winning Team USA at the Hlinka tournament last Summer, finishing second on the team.  He reminds me of Ryan Nugent-Hopkins’ game, as a smart, pass-first, two-way forward with a lot of versatility.  Sign me up for that.      









2#23: Gleb Pugachyov, 6’3 LW, RUS:  



While the Sabres have a certain profile they look for in many of their higher-end prospects, one area they don’t seem to focus on power forwards.  So it’s time to add one of those to the pipeline.  And this kid certainly checks those boxes.  We also know that Jarmo has had good success drafting big Russian forwards (Marchenko, Voronkov, Dolzhenkov, and Chinakov) in the middle rounds during his previous gig, so we’re drawing on that as well.  Pugachyov is a true bull-in-a-china shop player, in the same way that, say, Beck Malenstyn can be.  Extremely physical, loves to hit and invites contact all over the ice.  A true Russian bear.  But there’s plenty more to his game than just blowing guys up.  Has the hands and the head to try things as his game evolves and he grows more comfortable.  



A rangy 6’3, he plays with a longer stick and has pretty good reach on his own, which makes his ability to possess the puck – especially in transition – very dangerous.  He’s tough to take the puck off.  Really good habits, solid details. Makes a beeline for the net when he doesn’t have the puck, creates massive screens with his width and wingspan, and can carry defenders with him.  Even occasionally “bumps” the goalie when moving from post-to-post.  Has enough ability with his stick to deflect point shots into the back of the net, and his balance and sheer strength let him use his nose for the net to find and score loose pucks around the crease.  



While he doesn’t consistently show his skill level, he does have flashes where you see how much potential he has, dangling through defenders on an end-to-end rush or slipping a beautiful pass between two defenders for a streaking teammate to get a great scoring chance.  Has a quick release on a really hard snapshot.  Skating for Pugachyov is high-end in terms of straight lines.  He can really fly, again like Malenstyn, and builds up a real head of steam.  But his agility needs some work.  Sometimes he goes through guys because he just doesn’t evade opponents as quickly as someone else.  Still, he gets up and down the ice in a flash.  



For a young Russian forward, he has good instincts in his own end, seems to recognize patterns and defends the high-danger areas well while winning his share of wall battles even against seasoned pros.  Very good at using his size against opposing forwards around the front of the net, and both his length and strength makes it tough for forwards to get around him.  As with most quality Russian prospects, The Pug bounced up and down from Russian juniors (MHL) to the top of the heap (KHL) at various points this year.  



He finished with 24P in 36 games, with 9P in 16 playoff games, for the MHL’s Chaika Nihzny, but also put up 3P in 13 games for the parent club, Torpedo Nihzny, in the KHL…no small feat for a 17-year-old kid.  I think you could get a Kieffer Sherwood-sort of player with enough development window for this guy, a physical powerhouse who becomes more comfortable showing a little offensive flair and the ability to get to the net as he ages.             









4#30:  Tim Runtso, 6’2 RHD, WHL **:  



A kid who’s come from off-the-radar to a rising prospect in just 18 months, Runtso went from a Tier II Junior Kings program, to the NAHL, to the WHL in a flash and just keeps improving with each stop.  This season, playing for the Victoria Royals and slotting into the spot vacated by top prospect Keaton Verhoeff (who went to the NCAA), he quickly assumed top pair duties and surprisingly thrived.  Runtso is an offensive-minded blueliner.  He attacks the puck before it gets to the blue line, pinches hard, carries the puck below the circles and if he gets a step, all the way to the crease.  



Expert at the give-and-go, he manages to free himself up enough to get open into the low slot surprisingly often.  With the puck on his stick, he’s got a lot of craftiness in his game.  Subtle head and shoulder fakes, look-offs, and enough stickhandling to beat his check and attack deeper in the O-zone.  Likewise, in the Neutral, he will carry or pass the puck to create odd-man rushes, and typically puts the puck in the right place at the right time, on the tape.  Has no conscience with the puck, which can be a benefit and a detriment.  Doesn’t have a cannon from the point, but gets pucks through to the goal and can launch a heavy snapshot from the top of the zone, or toe-drag into the middle before ripping an accurate wrister.  



Where he needs work is on the defensive end.  Rarely steps up at his own blue line.  Can concede the entry and then finds himself backing up too deep.  As you can see, his transition defense needs plenty of work.  Can be taken outside-in, and beat to the middle, as he doesn’t do enough to kill plays or drive the puck carrier into low-danger areas.  But as a one-v-one defender, his on-puck defense has improved immeasurably over the course of this season.  Which is saying something because his footwork still needs to improve.  He can get pretzeled by an especially talented stickhandler coming down on the rush and left in the dust.  So there’s some work there left to do.  That said, Runtso plays the kind of game that, with some coaching, fits nicely with how the Sabres like to play.  That should be appealing to Jarmo.  



Was the leading rookie D-Man scorer in the WHL, with 44P in 68 games.  Off to a rising Miami (OH) program next season, where he’ll play with a number of current NHL prospects.  Given the Sabres’ organizational depth on defense, they can be patient with this kid as his game evolves.  And at the end, they could have a John Klingberg-type of player.  Not bad for the 4thround.       









5#30: Liam Lefebvre, 6’3 C, QMJHL**:  



Another over-ager, this big, reliable centerman is a bit of a late bloomer.  Last year at this time, he was playing at a US prep school.  Now he’s centering the 2nd line at the Memorial Cup for Quebec League champion Chicoutimi.  A big boy at 6’3 205#, Lefebvre has some explosive offensive capability and some high-level skating to fall back on.  Has exceptional lateral agility, allowing him to change directions on a dime, slide to evade bodies or defenders and open up passing or shooting lanes.  It also permits him to be a very dangerous puck possession forward, able to slip checks or hold off defenders with his size and reach while he scans the ice for open teammates.  



Amazingly at his size, he’s slippery and can get lost in transition as he moves easily, but purposefully, to open ice.  Loves to cut to the middle with the puck and break down the defense.  Can get a little overconfident with the puck, over-handling it and getting himself caught on the wrong side of the puck.  This traps him if he turns it over, which he does more frequently than I’d like.  Yet he’s very dangerous with the puck on his stick, distributing quick passes to open teammates or using them as decoys to load up a heavy wrister that he uses everywhere between the dots.  Has tremendous strength and balance.  



Able to push away smaller defenders, while able to take a hit and hang on to the puck in the process, he’s a bit of a freight train coming down the tracks when he’s really moving with the puck.  Defensively, he’s fairly accomplished.  Not great, but rarely makes the big mistake.  Doesn’t get caught out of position, works hard at keeping opponents out of the scoring areas, and his active stick and reach makes him tough in one-v-one defense.  Really good in puck recovery as well.  As a rookie, started with Rimouski but was traded mid-year to Chicoutimi, where he’s been better than a PPG (30P in 25 games) and has been extremely effective in the playoffs (12P in 20 games) as they won the Quebec League and are now battling for the Memorial Cup.  Off to the University of Vermont next season.  If he hits his ceiling, you’re looking at a big, power-center in the mold of a Tomas Hertl.   









6#30:  Pavel Denisov, 6’2 LHD, RUS:  



As we pointed out before, we know Jarmo likes to pick over the Russian leagues later in the Draft looking for diamonds-in-the-rough.  That’s how he found Vladislav Gavrikov in the 6thround.  Here he goes with someone with a little more offensive punch in Denisov.  A highly mobile, smooth skating defender who thinks defense first, Denisov has a smart two-way game and plays important minutes when the game is on the line for the #2 team in the Russian Junior league.  With a full toolbox, this kid can play the game any way you want.  High-paced chaos, he can get up ice with his skating and attack gaps and seams to break down the defense.  Slow, methodical checking game?  He can gap up, play a heavy game, and get the puck to safety consistently.  A bruising, heavy forechecking game?  He’s mobile enough to escape the forecheck and is more than happy to lay some big hits on the opposition.  



Good 4-way skating, transitions easily, and can either pass or carry the puck through the Neutral Zone with some authority.  Not a lot of creativity in his game, however.  Offensive game is simple, but effective.  Exits the zone like a mini-Owen Power, with crisp passes always snapped with velocity but easy enough to handle and usually on the tape.  Does a great job of springing transition.  On the other end, textbook transition defending.  Pushes oncoming forwards into low-danger areas, leaves his check to protect the net front, and uses a violent stick to knock pucks out of trouble or disrupt a player trying to dangle him.  



One of the youngest players in the Draft Class (missed the cut-off for next year’s Draft by two weeks!), Denisov’s already gotten called up to the VHL, the Russian second tier Men’s League, for 12 games this year and acquitted himself well.  Looked good in Russian juniors, where he put up 16P in 40 games on one of their top teams.  Next year he’s scheduled to start in the VHL.  Denisov isn’t really elite at any one thing, but his game is so composed, versatile and well-rounded, he could develop into just about any sort of defender.  Given the Sabres’ depth at the position, they can be patient with him and see what he becomes.

stories.title: Letter Grades for the Players, 2025-26 Season – Part 1 of 2
stories.summary:
After Great Team Success, How Do the Sabres Break Down Individually?



Today we take a look at the individual performances of each player and assign a letter grade accordingly. Each Sabre graded must meet the 20 games played with Buffalo minimum. So no Konsta Helenius or Sam Carrick and several others. These grades are for the regular season only, not playoffs. We will split the eligible players into two randomly listed sections. This will be part 1 of 2.




Owen Power (81-8-21-29 +9) Grade B




Power made some strides this season with his defensive game, especially late in the year. (and on into the playoffs) He provided a bit more physicality into the middle pairing and seems to close the gap quicker on his opponent with tighter coverage. His offense fell off to just 29 points but I feel that will bump up again soon as he rounds out his game. Lots to like here as Power continues to grow.




Beck Malenstyn (81-7-7-14 even) Grade B+




Malenstyn had himself an impressive season, being a rock on the 4th line with his heavy hitting game. He provided a strong forecheck and a grinding, shutdown performance while scoring some spectacular goals along the way. I love his effort level every night, especially blocking shots.




Jordan Greenway (40-1-5-6 -10) Grade C-




It’s hard to come down too hard on Greenway, knowing how difficult his season was. His injury really limited his games and performance until late in the season when we saw a healthy, rejuvenated player. His treatments and therapy were substantial. Still, we hoped for much more. Playoff Greenway gives us hope, if he can stay on the right track.




Bowen Byram (82-11-31-42 +15) Grade B+




Good stuff all around from Bo this season. He was the only defenseman to play all 82 games and provided a great blend of offense and defense. He was solid and dependable. I sure do hope the Sabres can extend him at a reasonable cost. Unfortunately, Byram seems like his aspirations are beyond what Buffalo can offer.




Michael Kesselring (34-0-2-2 even) Grade D




Kesselring had a season to forget, dealing with injury most of the year and never really settling in with the team. He became a forgotten man at the end of the season and that was a shame. I feel Kess still has much to offer and I’m not ready to move on from him like some are. He was supposedly the main piece for Buffalo in the JJ Peterka trade. If we get him going again along with Josh Doan’s early success then the Sabres are in business.




Jason Zucker (62-24-21-45 -5) Grade B+




Zuck scored 24 goals while missing 20 games, so you can’t complain too much about that. He was an offensively effective middle six player for Buffalo and brought that veteran leadership to the team. At 34 years old now, he could be pushed out by the likes of highly skilled Noah Ostlund and Konsta Helenius soon. Him being dealt would also make room for Buffalo with the tight cap. I still like the player though and I thought he had a strong season.




Zach Benson (65-13-30-43- +27) Grade A-




Taz is a major league pest whose reputation is expanding across the NHL, especially after the playoffs. He is a hard working, relentless agitator who is a smooth playmaker and goes to the net to score the dirty goals. His motor is non-stop and he is in everybody’s top three Buffalo Sabres players’ list. A full season next year should see Benny push for 60 points or more and become a fixture on the top line. The sky is the limit for this 21 year old who plays like a grizzled vet.



Apr 15, 2026; Buffalo, New York, USA; Buffalo Sabres left wing Zach Benson (6) scores a goal against Dallas Stars goaltender Jake Oettinger (29) in the second period at KeyBank Center. Mandatory Credit: Mark Konezny-Imagn Images




Alex Lyon (36-20 W-10 L-2.77-.907-3 SH) Grade B+




All things considered, Alex Lyon was a successful addition to the team. 20 wins with 3 shutouts is nothing to sneeze at. He mostly shared a rotation with UPL but did go off to start several in a row at times. He was a steady presence all year (especially on the road) and a good teammate. It was unfortunate things imploded for him in round 2 after a great showing against Boston.




Jack Quinn (82-20-31-51- +4 ) Grade B-




Jack set career highs this season in games played, goals, assists and points, yet he left most of us wanting more. I suppose that is because of his many dry spells, as he tends to score in bunches. There IS more talent there to uncork for this young man, he is just on a slow development curve it seems. He needs more consistency in his game. He did make strides in his defense, providing a stronger backcheck.




Ryan McLeod (81-14-40-54- +25) Grade B




Toothless didn’t score as many goals this year with just 14 but his overall points increased by 1 over last season. He was also the Sabres’ 4th leading scorer in 2025-26, something many fans may not realize. His 54 points, along with his great speed and strong penalty killing make him a valuable commodity. I hope he remains a 3rd line fixture in Buffalo for many years to come.




Zach Metsa (43-2-4-6- +16) Grade B




Metsa made a big splash this year as the 27 year old “rookie” who had his own cult following. His performance was near insanity for a while, as the other team just could not score when he was on the ice. This went on for a few months before the magic finally wore off. But Zach made a lasting impression and could serve Buffalo well in a continued bottom-six roll or as the Sabres’ 7th defenseman. He is cool and calm and makes the smart play.




Rasmus Dahlin (77-19-55-74- +18) Grade A




Rasmus set a career high in points and plus/minus in a season filled with personal trauma that overshadowed his hockey career. He overcame it to the tune of personal bests, a Norris Trophy nomination, (awarded annually to the NHL’s best defenseman) and a return to the playoffs after 14 long years for the Sabres. Not too shabby as his fiance continues her recovery with his support. On the ice, Dahlin still takes too many penalties and still makes the occasional bad turnover but he has matured into one of the best overall defenders in the league.



​Part 2 will be coming soon.

stories.title: 2026 UFL thread.
stories.summary:
1. 60+ yard field goals = 4 points
	2.  One foot in bounds = catch
	3. Red zone penalties revert to half the distance to the goal. 
	4. The "Tush Push" is eliminated.
	5. No punting inside the opponent’s 50-yard line.
	6. Overtime: 3 alternating attempts per team from the 5 yard line.
	 
	7. Point aftertouchdown options:
	- 1 point – Kick from the 33-yard line
	- 2 points – Ball on the 2-yard line
	- 3 points – Ball on the 8-yard line



	 



	Season starts Friday, March 27.

stories.title: Former 1st round #12 pick Henry Ruggs: 3 to 10 yrs prison for drunk driving fatal crash
stories.summary:
https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/38162272/ex-raiders-wr-henry-ruggs-iii-sentenced-3-10-years-fatal-dui



	 



	
		Quote
	

	
		
			Ruggs, 24, had been under house arrest with alcohol and location electronic monitoring devices since pleading guilty in May to one count of DUI resulting in death and one count of misdemeanor vehicular manslaughter in the crash that killed Tina Tintor and her dog, Max. On Wednesday, Ruggs read from a statement directed at Tintor's family in which he apologized for his actions.
		

		
			"To the parents and family of Ms. Tintor, I sincerely apologize for the pain and suffering," Ruggs said.
		
	



	 



	Pretty sure Ms Tintor's family don't think 3 years is nearly enough punishment for burning her alive.
	
	Ruggs got what his attorneys asked for:



	
		Quote
	

	
		
			In court documents filed before the sentencing hearing, Ruggs' attorneys had asked the judge to keep the duration of their client's prison time to a range of three to 10 years. Chesnoff and Richard Schonfeld said Ruggs "wishes that he could turn back time and change the outcome of the tragic events of November 2, 2021."
		
	



	 



	I'm sure he does, what with being cut from the team and losing his career and all.  But he can't.
